review_method: AI-assisted evidence pass plus a separate policy pass; no independent human reviewer is claimed

label_errors.reason: A standalone observational driving joke contains no solicitation, charge, contact instruction, or commercial intent; both OOF models and ham neighbours oppose the spam label.
The Divorce Barbie line is a self-contained joke with no spam action; both OOF text models strongly predict ham.
The child-versus-teenager line is a conversational joke with no commercial or contact mechanism; both OOF models strongly predict ham.
The stolen-toilet line is a one-line joke rather than a solicitation; both OOF models strongly oppose the spam label.
The Arsenal dartboard line is a joke with no transaction details or response route; both OOF models strongly predict ham.
The sender warns the recipient about a scam and says not to respond; the spam-like terms are quoted as safety context, and both OOF models predict ham.
The message asks whether received dating texts are connected to a prior action; it discusses spam rather than soliciting the recipient, and both OOF models predict ham.
This is a named personal debt-collection update thanking the recipient for information, not an offer; both OOF models strongly predict ham.
The tattoo line is a standalone joke without solicitation; both trained models strongly predict ham, but held-out labels remain untouched.
The wording reads as a recipient asking what to do after a winning-number message rather than originating the promotion; both models predict ham.
An unsolicited contact-platform invitation promotes free SMS and supplies an opt-out command; both OOF models predict spam.

ambiguous.reason: The message could be personal flirting or an unsolicited adult-chat lure; no explicit price or service identifier resolves the sender relationship.
The text resembles a personal missed-call follow-up but includes a 070 number often used in premium-rate lures; sender context is unavailable.
Product-trial recruitment requests address and date of birth, but could be an expected research invitation or unsolicited promotion.
A charity donation call-to-action is promotional and billable but socially legitimate; the corpus policy does not state whether opt-in charity campaigns count as spam.
A club ticket-sale notice may be expected by members or unsolicited commercial advertising; relationship context is missing.
A job-description delivery notice may be an expected recruiter follow-up, while the generic contact wording resembles bulk recruitment messaging.
A contact-list invitation advertises a free-SMS website but may be generated by a known contact; consent and sender context are unavailable.
The short statement about liking a mobile triggered model uncertainty but is plainly compatible with ordinary ham; uncertainty is not semantic ambiguity.

shortcut_examples.reason: The shallow model predicts spam at 0.991 because of price and digits, while the full text model predicts ham; monetary cues cause a clear false positive.
Recruitment language plus contact details produces a 0.900 shallow spam score although the public label and text model are ham.
The shallow model misses a ringtone-club solicitation because it lacks the usual numeric cues, while the full text model predicts spam.
A mobile-club offer is a shallow-model false negative, showing that contact/digit rules do not cover all spam templates.
A premium-rate adult-chat lure is detected almost entirely by phone, price, and contact markers while the full text model misses it.
Premium-price and reply markers drive the shallow spam probability to approximately 1.0 even though the text model is below threshold.
URL and free-SMS language push the shallow model toward spam on a public ham label, exposing a policy-sensitive shortcut.
A spam-labelled recipient reply has almost no shallow spam markers, demonstrating how surface cues interact with likely label noise.
